=== prep/README ===
# Uber SDE-2 Prep Kit (Python) — Self-Contained

Everything here is generated from analysis of 44 real Uber loops (Jun 2025–Jun 2026,
12 offers / 32 rejections) + ~700 LeetCode Discuss posts. It works **standalone or
with any AI model** — nothing depends on a specific chat session.

## Folder map

| Folder | What | How to use |
|---|---|---|
| `learn/` | **10 teaching docs** — each pattern from zero: intuition → template → worked Uber example → mistakes that cost offers → practice ladder | START HERE when something "samajh nahi aata". Read the doc, then do its mock/drill. |
| `deepdives/` | **14 question-level deep dives with Mermaid diagrams** — one per mock: full reasoning path, worked traces, and EVERY follow-up answered in detail | Mock first, then read — pass or fail. The "agar nahi aaya toh yahan se seekh lo" layer. |
| `mocks/` | **15 mock-interview kits** (5 LLD, 5 DSA, 4 HLD, 1 HM) + retake problems in every kit | Paste the `*_INTERVIEWER.md` into ANY model → it becomes a calibrated Uber interviewer with the real follow-ups and grading rubric. Code in the matching `.py`. See `mocks/README.md`. |
| `solutions/` | Editorial Python solutions for every Tier-1/Tier-2 repeated question, **with the exact follow-up variants Uber asked** (all runnable + tested) | Attempt first, then read. `python3 <file>` runs its tests. |
| `lld/` | Reference implementations of the 5 recurring machine-coding problems, thread-safe, with follow-up answers at the bottom of each file | Do the timed mock first, then diff your code against these. |
| `hld/` | End-to-end playbooks for the 5 HLD archetypes, in the exact format Uber grades (incl. "sentences that score") | Read one per day in week 3; rehearse out loud; then run the matching mock. |
| `behavioral/` | STAR Work Document template built from the real HM question data | Fill it completely in writing — an offer-getter credited exactly this for selection. |
| `cribsheets/` | **One page per round** (OA, DSA, LLD, HLD, HM) — protocol, minute marks, the rules from real debriefs | The ONLY thing to read on interview day. |
| `PHRASE_BANK.md` | Exact English sentences for every interview moment — clarifying, narrating, complexity, stuck, follow-ups, STAR | Rehearse out loud until automatic; fluency under pressure is its own skill. |
| `PLAN.md` | 4-week schedule + 15-mock log | Tick boxes; log verdicts after each mock. |

**Question bank (`../index.html`) now has progress tracking:** every card has
✓ Done / ★ Revisit buttons (saved in your browser), a Done counter in the
header, and a Progress filter (Done / Revisit / Untouched) inside Filters —
use it to systematically clear all 859 questions.

## Which doc when (quick router)

| "Mujhe ___ samajh nahi aata" | Read |
|---|---|
| Union-Find / connectivity | `learn/01` |
| BFS, grids, multi-source, binary search on answer | `learn/02` |
| Alien dictionary / ordering from constraints | `learn/03` |
| Heaps, top-K, streams | `learn/04` |
| Sliding window, next-greater | `learn/05` |
| DP (interval / game / grid) | `learn/06` |
| Palindrome questions | `learn/07` |
| How to even DO the LLD round | `learn/08` |
| Locks, threads, GIL, Condition | `learn/09` |
| How to even DO the HLD round | `learn/10` |

content.text:
I've now got the complete, current picture — recipe, prompt structure, frontend contract, serving path, **and** the full inventory of what already exists. Here's where things stand.

## Yes — and "properly" is fully specified now

The recipe from this session is locked and verified against the live code:

- **Working tested code** → flat layout (`models.py`, optional `strategies.py`/`gateways.py`, `<domain>_service.py`, `main.py`, `tests/`), Python 3.11+ idioms, single domain exception base, clean single-threaded core + `ThreadSafe<Domain>Service` subclass via the no-op `_lock` hook.
- **Full §1–§9 markdown** (⏱️ 60-min plan → clarifying dialogue → entities accept/reject → class design → earned patterns → algorithms → narrated impl → verified run → sequence diagram → concurrency+twists).
- **Frontend-openable conversation JSON** → exact `write_file`/`path`(bare) + `run_command`/`command` tool-block contract, written to `data/conversations/<slug>.json`, opens at `https://localhost:8002/?s=<uuid>:lld` with the inline tabbed "Complete Code Implementation" viewer.
- **A distinct concurrency pattern per problem**, with the strict `Barrier` race test asserting **unique resources**, not just a win-count.

## What already exists (so I don't duplicate)

~14 distinct topics already shipped: **Parking Lot, Movie Ticket, Splitwise, API Rate Limiter, Ride Sharing, Message Broker (Pub-Sub), Task Scheduler, Leaderboard, Meeting Room, Notification Router, Coupon, Vending Machine, Elevator, TTL KV Store** (plus Amazon Locker, Idempotent Order Engine, Food Ordering, File System APIs in older files).

Note: `uber-interviews/prep/lld/` is **separate** standalone study material (5 reference `.py` files) — not the frontend session system. New problems go to `data/conversations/` as `_lld.json`.

## Proposed new problems — each teaches a concurrency pattern *not yet used*

| # | Problem | Distinct concurrency angle |
|---|---------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Connection / Resource Pool** | bounded **semaphore** + blocking acquire-with-timeout + fair return |
| 2 | **Thread Pool / Worker Executor** | bounded blocking queue + worker threads + graceful **poison-pill** shutdown + futures |
| 3 | **Order Matching Engine** (stock exchange) | per-symbol order book, **price-time priority**, partial fills under concurrent orders |
| 4 | **Thread-Safe LRU Cache** | **lock striping** (sharded locks) + O(1) DLL eviction |
| 5 | **Snowflake ID Generator** | atomic sequence + **clock-tick spin-wait** + sequence rollover |
| 6 | **Online Auction** | optimistic **CAS** on highest bid + soft-close auto-extend |
| 7 | **Distributed Lock / Lease Manager** | lease TTL + **fencing tokens** + expiry sweeper |

Each is a real Uber-style machine-coding problem and fills a genuine gap in your concurrency vocabulary.

Let me confirm scope before I spend the tokens generating — picking the right problems for your Uber prep is your call:

questions.question: Which new LLD problems should I generate? (Each is a full session: tested code + §1–§9 markdown + frontend-openable JSON.)

options.description: Bounded semaphore + blocking acquire-with-timeout. Classic 'borrow/return' concurrency primitive — high-value, not yet covered.

options.label: Thread Pool Executor
options.description: Bounded queue + worker threads + graceful poison-pill shutdown + futures. The producer-consumer + worker-pool pattern.

options.label: Order Matching Engine
options.description: Per-symbol order book, price-time priority, partial fills. Rich domain, concurrent matching — strong senior signal.

options.label: Thread-Safe LRU Cache
options.description: Lock striping (sharded locks) + O(1) doubly-linked-list eviction. Extremely common Uber-style ask.

message.content:
ek issue hai prompt me LLD ke :


3. Core Entities (with explicit accept / reject reasoning)
3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough (accept / reject one by one)
Elevator (car) — A real physical thing with an id, a current floor, a direction, a door, and a set of floors it still owes a visit. It has both data AND behaviour: it decides how to move (SCAN). This is the only genuinely interesting object. Accepted — and it's a state machine.
ElevatorController — Something has to own the bank of cars, route hall calls to a car, and drive the simulation. That coordination doesn't belong on any single car. Accepted — the orchestrator.
DispatchStrategy — "Which car answers this hall call?" is a pure policy decision the building will want to tune. Pulling it into its own type lets the controller stay ignorant of the policy. Accepted — as an interface (the extensibility seam).
Request (hall call) — A hall call carries a floor + a direction and is reasoned about by the strategy. It has no behaviour, but it's a meaningful immutable bundle that crosses a boundary. Accepted as a small frozen value object — not a rich entity.
Floor — Tempting to make a Floor class. But our system only cares about a floor number; there's no per-floor state or behaviour worth a class. Rejected — it's just an int bounded by [min_floor, max_floor].
Button — A button press is an action (it calls request_elevator or select_floor), not stored state. Rejected — modelled as a method call, not an object.
Door — Has exactly two values (OPEN / CLOSED) and no behaviour of its own beyond the car opening/closing it. Rejected as entity — a DoorState enum field on Elevator.
Direction — A finite set UP / DOWN / IDLE. Rejected as entity — an enum that's shared vocabulary.
Passenger — Calls the system; the system never stores a passenger. Rejected — an actor, not an entity.
Building — Just supplies min_floor/max_floor config; no runtime behaviour. Rejected — two ints on the controller.
StopEvent — Not from the requirements directly, but the natural output of the simulation (doors opened at floor F on tick T). Worth a tiny immutable record so the trace is typed instead of tuples. Accepted as a value object — for the demo/test trace only.
3.2 Final entity table (accepted entities only)
Entity    Responsibility    Key fields (typed)    Invariant it guards
ElevatorController    Route hall calls, accept car calls, drive ticks    _elevators: dict[str, Elevator] (registry by id), _dispatch: DispatchStrategy, _min_floor/_max_floor: int, _tick: int    Every public call is validated before any car is mutated
Elevator    Move itself via SCAN; remember its stops    id: str (id), current_floor: int, direction: Direction, state: ElevatorState, door: DoorState, _up_stops/_down_stops: set[int]    Only it mutates its own movement; a stop is queued at most once; SCAN order is honoured
DispatchStrategy (abstract)    Pick one car for a hall call    (interface — no state)    Never returns a car in maintenance
Request (frozen)    Carry a hall call to the strategy    floor: int, direction: Direction    Immutable once created
StopEvent (frozen)    Record a doors-opened event    tick: int, elevator_id: str, floor: int, direction: Direction    Immutable trace record
Enums: Direction { UP, DOWN, IDLE } · ElevatorState { IDLE, MOVING, DOORS_OPEN, MAINTENANCE } · DoorState { OPEN, CLOSED }

3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro
"OK so the model has effectively four objects. ElevatorController is the brain — it owns the bank of cars and the dispatch policy, and it drives the simulation tick by tick. Elevator is the only interesting one: it's a state machine that owns its own movement — where it is, which way it's going, and the floors it still owes — and it implements SCAN itself, so the controller never reaches in and nudges a car by hand. DispatchStrategy is the seam: 'which car answers?' is a swappable policy. Request and StopEvent are tiny frozen value objects — one carries a hall call in, the other records a stop coming out. The things I deliberately left OUT are Floor, Button, Door and Passenger — those are an int, a method call, an enum, and an actor respectively. None of them owns state worth a class."

content.content:
=== All section headers in lld_prompt.md (lines with ## or numbered sections) ===
84:IMPORTANT — this is a toolbox, not a checklist. For each problem, pick the ones that actually fit. Using all 8 on a Chess problem would look forced and wrong. Also: after picking from this list, ask yourself what the "smart move" is SPECIFIC TO THIS PROBLEM — every LLD has one non-obvious insight the interviewer most wants to hear. State it clearly (in §5.5-A) and make the code reflect it.
248:Every LLD problem has one non-obvious insight that is specific to that problem. This is the thing the interviewer most wants to hear — the moment that makes them think "this person actually understands this domain." State it in §5.5-A and make the code show it. Here are examples, so you know the style:
272:| `strategies.py` | Strategy interfaces + concrete impls (allocation, pricing, etc.). | only if a Strategy pattern is in §5 |
273:| `gateways.py` | External-system interfaces (PaymentGateway, NotificationSink, Clock). | only if §5 names an external dependency |
401:| 0–5 min | **Say §1-2 out loud** — restate problem, ask clarifying questions. Do not write yet. | 🗣️ SAY |
402:| 5–12 min | **Draw the core class diagram** — ONLY the 5-6 boxes that matter (see §4). Verbally mention the simple ones (Theatre, Movie, Screen, User) in one sentence, do not draw them. | ✍️ DRAW |
403:| 12–14 min | **State patterns + concurrency flag** — name your patterns (§5), then immediately flag the race condition: "I see a check-then-hold in select_seats that's a race — I'll show how I'd fix it at the end." | 🗣️ SAY |
404:| 14–18 min | **Walk §5.5 approach** — state the key insight, explain select_seats algorithm in plain words. | 🗣️ SAY |
405:| 18–38 min | **Write code in interview order** (see §6 — follow the ✍️ WRITE sequence exactly): enums → ShowSeat state machine → BookingService skeleton → select_seats() → make_payment() | ✍️ WRITE |
408:| 55–60 min | **Follow-up questions** — use the hardest follow-ups from §9. | ❓ IF ASKED |
411:- **Mode A — Whiteboard / no-run (Amazon, Google):** Skip §7 run output. Demo cases verbally. Write code but do not execute.
412:- **Mode B — Coding environment (Uber, Meta, startups):** Write and RUN main.py in §7. Show actual terminal output. Cases are proved by the program.
442:INTERVIEWER REPLIES — keep them short, realistic, and DIVERSE: ~70% pure agreement ("yep" / "sounds good" / "agreed"), ~20% small refinement ("agreed, but also handle case W"), ~10% a clean pushback the candidate handles gracefully ("actually I'd include retries" → italic line: "OK — I'll add a retry policy in §9 instead of cutting it"). Avoid robotic tone — sound like a real PM/tech lead.
444:ITALIC REACTION LINES — short, decisive, forward-looking. Either lock in the assumption ("✓ scoped: one package per compartment, no bulk pickups") or note the fallback ("if they wanted multi-region, I'd shard by station_id — flag for §9"). Never robotic recap of what was just said.
461:GROUNDING NOTE: do NOT make up requirements the interviewer never gave. Every functional requirement must trace back to either the original prompt or a Q&A turn in §1.2. If the candidate adds an assumption (e.g. "I'll assume codes are 6-digit numeric"), it must be stated explicitly with "I'll assume…" — never silently introduced.
464:A SHORT section — this is just framing for the entity work in §3. Two parts only.
473:Each flow = ONE LINE. No nested bullets, no sub-steps. The detailed flow lives in §6 (implementation walkthrough) and §8 (sequence diagram). This section just confirms WHAT the system supports so §3's entities have a clear motivation.
475:DO NOT draw a system-context diagram or sequence diagram here. §8 has the sequence diagram for the most interesting flow; one diagram is enough. This section is text-only.
481:List EVERY noun from §1.3 requirements (the obvious ones — Package, Compartment, Locker, Driver, Customer, AccessToken, Code, Expiry, Size, etc.). For EACH noun, write a SHORT prose paragraph (2-3 sentences) explaining:
502:Only the entities accepted in §3.1 appear here. Each row:
515:GROUNDING NOTE: every entity in §3.2 must show up unchanged in the §4 class diagram and the §6 code. If you're tempted to add an entity here that won't have any code in §6, either delete it or move it to §3.1's REJECTED list with a reason.
522:For EACH entity accepted in §3, write a focused subsection in this exact order:
530:**(b) State derivation TABLE** — derives fields directly from §1.3 requirements:
547:**(c) Operations derivation TABLE** — derives methods directly from §1.3 requirements:
569:Repeat this whole (a)→(d) block for every accepted entity from §3. Keep the subsections SHORT — 4-block structure is mandatory but each block is tight. The point is to show DERIVATION and DEFENSIBLE CHOICES, not to write a textbook.
581:A `classDiagram` Mermaid block containing ONLY the ✍️ draw-these classes — this is THE canonical design and the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH. This exact diagram MUST match §3's entities, §4.1's per-class blocks, AND the §6/§7 code one-for-one — identical class names, fields, and relationships. Do not draw a class or field you won't implement, and do not implement one that isn't on the diagram.
604:⚠️ **PROACTIVE CONCURRENCY FLAG — say this immediately after presenting the diagram, before the interviewer asks:** "I notice there's a race condition in [main operation] — if two users call it at the same time, both see 'available', both proceed, we end up double-booking. I'll keep the core design clean and single-threaded for now, and show you exactly how I'd fix that in §9." This single sentence — said proactively, with confidence, before being asked — is the highest-value signal you can give an interviewer. It shows you have built real concurrent systems. Don't wait to be asked about concurrency. Say it right here.
616:For EACH PATTERN (only the ones genuinely used in §6 code):
630:This rule applies to every claim in §5.2:
631:- A pattern is "applied" ONLY if its interface/seam is visible in the §6/§7 code as a real interface with a real implementation, a real Observer registration, a real State transition. If the seam isn't in the code, it's not applied.

--- FILE: data/conversations/dbeae652-8333-4406-8f66-05b3c12a3f93_lld.json ---
## ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan
## 1. Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions
### 1.1 What we're building (plain words)
### 1.2 Clarifying questions (candidate-led proposals)
### 1.3 Final Requirements (the whiteboard summary)
## 2. Actors & Core Flows
## 3. Core Entities (with explicit accept / reject reasoning)
### 3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough (accept / reject one by one)
### 3.2 Final entity table (accepted entities only)
### 3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro
## 4. Class Design (per-class derivation, then the diagram)
### 4.1 Per-class derivation
#### `ElevatorController` (orchestrator — first)
# _lock(elevator) -> contextmanager (no-op in core)
#### `Elevator` (the state machine)
#### `DispatchStrategy` (the seam) + concretes
### 4.2 Final consolidated class diagram
## 5. Design Patterns & Principles
### 5.1 Information Expert
### 5.2 Applied patterns
### 5.3 SOLID
### 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS
## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach
### A. The Key Insight
### B. Per-Operation Algorithm
### C. Data Structure Choices
### D. Tricky Logic — claim, proof, fix
### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example
### F. Narration Order for §6
## 6. Implementation — narrated, class by class
### 6.1 `models.py` — vocabulary, errors, value objects
### 6.2 `models.py` — the `Elevator` state machine
### 6.3 `strategies.py` — the dispatch seam
### 6.4 `elevator_service.py` — the orchestrator (skeleton first)
## 7. Putting It Together (verified run + pytest suite)
### 7.1 `main.py` — narrated demo driver
# Case 1: SCAN sweeps up, then turns around
# Case 2: nearest-car dispatch answers a hall call
# Case 3: pressing the same floor twice is one stop
# Case 4: bad inputs -> typed errors  (InvalidFloor / InvalidDirection / UnknownElevator)
# Case 5: a maintenance car is skipped by dispatch
### 7.2 `tests/test_elevator.py` — pytest suite
## 8. Key Flow (sequence diagram)
## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility
# ─── §6 core: the no-op hook ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# ─── §9: thread-safe — diff is ONE overridden method ──────────────────────

Found it. Let me confirm what's actually happening, because the answer is a real gap — not a generation bug.

I traced the Elevator session you pasted (`dbeae652-...-_lld.json`) and listed every section it produced. Here's the actual order it generates:

```
§1 Understanding  →  §2 Actors & Flows  →  §3 Core Entities  →  §4 Class Design  →  §5 Patterns  →  §5.5 Algorithms  →  §6 Implementation  →  §7 Run  →  §8 Sequence  →  §9 Concurrency
```

**There is no API/Interface section in the prompt at all.** It jumps straight from §3 Core Entities → §4 Class Design. So it's not "failing to generate" — `lld_prompt.md` simply never defines one. I grepped every "API" mention in the prompt: they're all incidental ("read it like an API contract", "API tour", "public API for") — the API surface is currently *folded into* §4.1's operations table and §6's orchestrator skeleton, never shown as its own upfront section.

You're right that it *should* be there. The hellointerview ordering is **Requirements → Core Entities → API / System Interface → design** — the API section sits exactly where you said, right after §3. Ours skips it.

## The fix

Add a dedicated **API / System Interface** section right after §3 Core Entities — the public method contract of the orchestrator (signature, returns, raises, purpose) *before* diving into internal class structure. For the Elevator example it'd be:

```
§3.5  API / System Interface
"Before classes, I pin the public surface — what external code calls."

Method                              Signature                       Returns            Raises
request_elevator(floor, direction)  (int, Direction) -> str         assigned car id    InvalidFloor, InvalidDirection, NoCarAvailable
select_floor(car_id, floor)         (str, int) -> None              —                  UnknownElevator, InvalidFloor
tick()                              () -> list[StopEvent]           stops this tick    —
set_maintenance(car_id, on)         (str, bool) -> None             —                  UnknownElevator
```

I need your call on **placement**, because it affects how much of the prompt I touch:

477	## 3. Core Entities (with explicit accept / reject reasoning)
478	This is the MOST important section for showing object-modeling judgment. Many candidates fail by listing every noun as an entity without thinking. The hellointerview style — and what we want — is to enumerate EVERY candidate noun from the requirements and EXPLICITLY decide whether it earns entity status or gets rejected. A senior signal is rejecting a noun cleanly with a one-line reason. Three sub-parts:
479	
480	### 3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough (accept / reject one by one)
481	List EVERY noun from §1.3 requirements (the obvious ones — Package, Compartment, Locker, Driver, Customer, AccessToken, Code, Expiry, Size, etc.). For EACH noun, write a SHORT prose paragraph (2-3 sentences) explaining:
482	  - What this noun would represent if it were an entity
483	  - Whether it earns entity status — and the precise reason (does it own state? does it have behavior? is it referenced by multiple other entities?)
484	  - If REJECTED: where it lives instead (input parameter, field on another entity, out-of-scope external concept) — and ONE sentence on why that's the better home
485	
486	Format strictly:
487	
488	> **Package** — At first this seems obvious; we're storing packages. But our system only > cares about the package's SIZE — driver hands us a size, we pick a compartment. Package > ID, customer info, shipping details all live in Amazon's fulfillment system upstream. > *Rejected as entity — `size` is just an input parameter to deposit().*
489	>
490	> **Compartment** — A real physical thing with an ID, a size, and an occupancy state. > Has both data AND behavior (open the door, check if free). *Accepted.*
491	>
492	> **AccessToken** — Tempting to call this "just a string" and store it as a field on > Compartment. But a token has its OWN lifecycle (expiry timestamp, validation logic) and > represents a CAPABILITY (the right to open one specific compartment). That's worth its > own class — it lets AccessToken own the expiry check instead of scattering it. *Accepted.*
493	>
494	> **Driver / Customer** — Actors, not entities. They CALL the system; the system doesn't > store them. *Rejected — actors live outside the model.*
495	>
496	> **Size** — A finite set of values (SMALL/MEDIUM/LARGE) with no behavior. *Rejected as > entity — modeled as an enum.*
497	
498	Walk through 6-10 nouns in this style. The REJECTED ones are as important as the accepted ones — they show you thought about it.
499	
500	### 3.2 Final entity table (accepted entities only)
501	Compact table with columns: **Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) | Invariant it guards**.
502	Only the entities accepted in §3.1 appear here. Each row:
503	  - **Responsibility:** ONE sentence — the SINGLE thing this entity is responsible for.
504	  - **Key fields (typed):** the actual fields with types. Mark the id field. Mark references to other entities and explain (in parentheses) what that reference is FOR at runtime.
505	  - **Invariant it guards:** the one rule this entity enforces (e.g. "a Compartment can only transition AVAILABLE → RESERVED → OCCUPIED — illegal jumps are rejected").
506	
507	After the table, list **Enums** as a separate small block (e.g. `Size`, `Status`) with their values. Enums are not entities; they're shared vocabulary.
508	
509	### 3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro (the natural-language story)
510	A flowing PARAGRAPH (not bullets) that the candidate says out loud as they finish the table. Should sound like a senior engineer telling a colleague the story:
511	> "OK so the model has [N] objects. [OrchestratorClass] is the brain — it owns [collection] > and the [token map]. [EntityA] is the [physical/conceptual] thing — it remembers its own > [physical state] like [field]. [EntityB] is the interesting one because it owns [behavior]; > the orchestrator never duplicates that logic. The pieces I deliberately left OUT are > [rejected nouns] — those are [where they actually live]."
512	
513	The closing sentence about REJECTED nouns is what makes this paragraph senior — it tells the interviewer you considered them and decided. Don't skip it.
514	
515	GROUNDING NOTE: every entity in §3.2 must show up unchanged in the §4 class diagram and the §6 code. If you're tempted to add an entity here that won't have any code in §6, either delete it or move it to §3.1's REJECTED list with a reason.

517	## 4. Class Design (per-class derivation, then the diagram)
518	
519	This section has TWO halves. The first half DERIVES each class from the requirements one at a time — showing your work. The second half is the consolidated class diagram (the picture). Don't jump to the picture; the derivation is what shows judgment.
520	
521	### 4.1 Per-class derivation (orchestrator first, then the parts)
522	For EACH entity accepted in §3, write a focused subsection in this exact order:
523	  1. The ORCHESTRATOR (entry-point class) — first
524	  2. Then each supporting entity in dependency order (pieces it owns / references)
525	
526	For EACH class, the subsection has FOUR blocks in this order:
527	
528	**(a) One-line role** — what this class is the public API for / responsible for. > "The Locker is the system's public API — external code calls it to deposit and pick up. > Everything flows through here."
529	
530	**(b) State derivation TABLE** — derives fields directly from §1.3 requirements:
531	> | Requirement | What this class must remember |
532	> |---|---|
533	> | "System assigns an available compartment of matching size" | The collection of compartments |
534	> | "User retrieves package by entering access token" | A map: token code → AccessToken |
535	
536	Then write the resulting state block as a small fenced code block:
537	> ```
538	> class Locker:
539	>     - compartments: Compartment[]
540	>     - accessTokenMapping: Map<string, AccessToken>
541	> ```
542	
543	If a piece of state is NOT on this class but plausibly could be (e.g. occupancy could live on the orchestrator OR on the entity), add a short paragraph **"State placement trade-off"** that names both options and explains your choice — using the PHYSICAL vs RELATIONAL state heuristic:
544	> Physical state (contains a package, broken, needs maintenance) lives on the entity > because it describes the entity's CONDITION. Relational state (assigned to this token, > reserved by this user) lives in the orchestrator because it describes a system-managed > RELATIONSHIP. The key is having a rationale you can defend; both are valid for some > kinds of state.
545	Pick one and state your reasoning in one sentence.
546	
547	**(c) Operations derivation TABLE** — derives methods directly from §1.3 requirements:
548	> | Need from requirements | Method on this class |
549	> |---|---|
550	> | "Carrier deposits a package by specifying size" | `depositPackage(size) -> token \| error` |
551	> | "User retrieves package by entering access token" | `pickup(tokenCode) -> void \| error` |
552	
553	Then write the final class block (state + constructor + methods):

555	> class Locker:

558	>
559	>     + Locker(compartments)
560	>     + depositPackage(size) -> string | error

562	>     + openExpiredCompartments() -> void
563	> ```
564	
565	**(d) Design choices worth calling out** — 2-4 short Q&A bullets that pre-empt the "why did you do X?" interviewer questions. Each is a question + a 1-2 sentence answer. Use this format:

## 3. Core Entities (with explicit accept / reject reasoning)
This is the MOST important section for showing object-modeling judgment. Many candidates fail by listing every noun as an entity without thinking. The hellointerview style — and what we want — is to enumerate EVERY candidate noun from the requirements and EXPLICITLY decide whether it earns entity status or gets rejected. A senior signal is rejecting a noun cleanly with a one-line reason. Three sub-parts:

### 3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough (accept / reject one by one)
List EVERY noun from §1.3 requirements (the obvious ones — Package, Compartment, Locker, Driver, Customer, AccessToken, Code, Expiry, Size, etc.). For EACH noun, write a SHORT prose paragraph (2-3 sentences) explaining:
  - What this noun would represent if it were an entity
  - Whether it earns entity status — and the precise reason (does it own state? does it have behavior? is it referenced by multiple other entities?)
  - If REJECTED: where it lives instead (input parameter, field on another entity, out-of-scope external concept) — and ONE sentence on why that's the better home

Format strictly:

> **Package** — At first this seems obvious; we're storing packages. But our system only > cares about the package's SIZE — driver hands us a size, we pick a compartment. Package > ID, customer info, shipping details all live in Amazon's fulfillment system upstream. > *Rejected as entity — `size` is just an input parameter to deposit().*
>
> **Compartment** — A real physical thing with an ID, a size, and an occupancy state. > Has both data AND behavior (open the door, check if free). *Accepted.*
>
> **AccessToken** — Tempting to call this "just a string" and store it as a field on > Compartment. But a token has its OWN lifecycle (expiry timestamp, validation logic) and > represents a CAPABILITY (the right to open one specific compartment). That's worth its > own class — it lets AccessToken own the expiry check instead of scattering it. *Accepted.*
>
> **Driver / Customer** — Actors, not entities. They CALL the system; the system doesn't > store them. *Rejected — actors live outside the model.*
>
> **Size** — A finite set of values (SMALL/MEDIUM/LARGE) with no behavior. *Rejected as > entity — modeled as an enum.*

Walk through 6-10 nouns in this style. The REJECTED ones are as important as the accepted ones — they show you thought about it.

### 3.2 Final entity table (accepted entities only)
Compact table with columns: **Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) | Invariant it guards**.
Only the entities accepted in §3.1 appear here. Each row:
  - **Responsibility:** ONE sentence — the SINGLE thing this entity is responsible for.
  - **Key fields (typed):** the actual fields with types. Mark the id field. Mark references to other entities and explain (in parentheses) what that reference is FOR at runtime.
  - **Invariant it guards:** the one rule this entity enforces (e.g. "a Compartment can only transition AVAILABLE → RESERVED → OCCUPIED — illegal jumps are rejected").

After the table, list **Enums** as a separate small block (e.g. `Size`, `Status`) with their values. Enums are not entities; they're shared vocabulary.

### 3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro (the natural-language story)
A flowing PARAGRAPH (not bullets) that the candidate says out loud as they finish the table. Should sound like a senior engineer telling a colleague the story:
> "OK so the model has [N] objects. [OrchestratorClass] is the brain — it owns [collection] > and the [token map]. [EntityA] is the [physical/conceptual] thing — it remembers its own > [physical state] like [field]. [EntityB] is the interesting one because it owns [behavior]; > the orchestrator never duplicates that logic. The pieces I deliberately left OUT are > [rejected nouns] — those are [where they actually live]."

The closing sentence about REJECTED nouns is what makes this paragraph senior — it tells the interviewer you considered them and decided. Don't skip it.

GROUNDING NOTE: every entity in §3.2 must show up unchanged in the §4 class diagram and the §6 code. If you're tempted to add an entity here that won't have any code in §6, either delete it or move it to §3.1's REJECTED list with a reason.

## 4. Class Design (per-class derivation, then the diagram)

This section has TWO halves. The first half DERIVES each class from the requirements one at a time — showing your work. The second half is the consolidated class diagram (the picture). Don't jump to the picture; the derivation is what shows judgment.

### 4.1 Per-class derivation (orchestrator first, then the parts)
For EACH entity accepted in §3, write a focused subsection in this exact order:
  1. The ORCHESTRATOR (entry-point class) — first
  2. Then each supporting entity in dependency order (pieces it owns / references)

For EACH class, the subsection has FOUR blocks in this order:

**(a) One-line role** — what this class is the public API for / responsible for. > "The Locker is the system's public API — external code calls it to deposit and pick up. > Everything flows through here."

**(b) State derivation TABLE** — derives fields directly from §1.3 requirements:
> | Requirement | What this class must remember |
> |---|---|
> | "System assigns an available compartment of matching size" | The collection of compartments |
> | "User retrieves package by entering access token" | A map: token code → AccessToken |

Then write the resulting state block as a small fenced code block:
> ```
> class Locker:
>     - compartments: Compartment[]
>     - accessTokenMapping: Map<string, AccessToken>
> ```

If a piece of state is NOT on this class but plausibly could be (e.g. occupancy could live on the orchestrator OR on the entity), add a short paragraph **"State placement trade-off"** that names both options and explains your choice — using the PHYSICAL vs RELATIONAL state heuristic:
> Physical state (contains a package, broken, needs maintenance) lives on the entity > because it describes the entity's CONDITION. Relational state (assigned to this token, > reserved by this user) lives in the orchestrator because it describes a system-managed > RELATIONSHIP. The key is having a rationale you can defend; both are valid for some > kinds of state.
Pick one and state your reasoning in one sentence.

**(c) Operations derivation TABLE** — derives methods directly from §1.3 requirements:
> | Need from requirements | Method on this class |
> |---|---|
> | "Carrier deposits a package by specifying size" | `depositPackage(size) -> token \| error` |
> | "User retrieves package by entering access token" | `pickup(tokenCode) -> void \| error` |

Then write the final class block (state + constructor + methods):
> ```
> class Locker:
>     - compartments: Compartment[]
>     - accessTokenMapping: Map<string, AccessToken>
>
>     + Locker(compartments)
>     + depositPackage(size) -> string | error
>     + pickup(tokenCode) -> void | error
>     + openExpiredCompartments() -> void
> ```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out** — 2-4 short Q&A bullets that pre-empt the "why did you do X?" interviewer questions. Each is a question + a 1-2 sentence answer. Use this format:

## 3.5 API / System Interface (the public surface, before the classes)
This section is MANDATORY and comes BEFORE §4 — exactly the hellointerview ordering (Requirements → Core Entities → API/Interface → design). The point: once the entities exist, the candidate pins down the PUBLIC SURFACE of the system — the small set of methods external code actually calls — BEFORE designing internal class structure. Interviewers read this as "this person designs to a contract, not to an implementation." It is the single most-skipped section by weak candidates.

WHAT IT IS — the orchestrator's public methods ONLY. NOT internal/private helpers, NOT entity methods (those derive in §4.1). One row per public operation that an actor from §2 triggers. Every operation must trace back to a §1.3 functional requirement; if a requirement has no API method, you missed one — if an API method has no requirement, delete it.

Render it as ONE typed contract table with EXACTLY these columns:

> | Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | `request_elevator` | `(floor: int, direction: Direction) -> str` | id of the assigned car | `InvalidFloor`, `InvalidDirection`, `NoCarAvailable` | A passenger on a floor presses UP/DOWN; system picks a car |
> | `select_floor` | `(car_id: str, floor: int) -> None` | — | `UnknownElevator`, `InvalidFloor` | A passenger inside a car picks a destination |
> | `tick` | `() -> list[StopEvent]` | the stops that happened this tick | — | Advance the simulation one step (all cars move per SCAN) |
> | `set_maintenance` | `(car_id: str, on: bool) -> None` | — | `UnknownElevator` | Take a car in/out of service |

RULES for the table:
  - **Signature** uses real Python 3.11+ type hints (`list[X]`, `X | None`) — these signatures MUST match the §4.1 operations table, the §4.2 diagram, and the §6 code one-for-one. This table is a binding contract, not a sketch.
  - **Raises** lists the SPECIFIC typed exceptions (all subclasses of the one domain base from §3.2) — never bare `Exception`. Each distinct failure mode is its own row entry. A method that can't fail shows `—`.
  - **Returns** is the success value in plain words + the type. Use `—` for `None`.
  - **Purpose** is ONE plain-English line tying the method to the actor/flow from §2 — not a restatement of the signature.
  - Keep it to the 3-7 genuinely public methods. If the surface is bigger than ~7 methods, that's a smell — say so and group the secondary ones.

Close with ONE 🎙️ spoken line (not a paragraph) the candidate says as they finish the table — it should call out what the surface REVEALS about the design:
> 🎙️ "So the entire system is four public calls — two that take requests in (`request_elevator`, `select_floor`), one that drives time forward (`tick`), and one admin toggle. Everything else is internal: the cars move themselves, the strategy picks the car. If I were handed this interface cold, I'd know exactly what the system does without seeing a single class."

GROUNDING NOTE: every method in §3.5 MUST appear as a public method on the orchestrator in §4.1, in the §4.2 diagram, and in the §6 code — identical name and signature. The reverse also holds: the orchestrator has NO public method that isn't in this table. This table is the contract §4–§7 implement.

## 4. Class Design (per-class derivation, then the diagram)

> | `select_floor` | `(car_id: str, floor: int) -> None` | — | `UnknownElevator`, `InvalidFloor` | A passenger inside a car picks a destination |
> | `tick` | `() -> list[StopEvent]` | the stops that happened this tick | — | Advance the simulation one step (all cars move per SCAN) |
> | `set_maintenance` | `(car_id: str, on: bool) -> None` | — | `UnknownElevator` | Take a car in/out of service |

RULES for the table:
  - **Signature** uses real Python 3.11+ type hints (`list[X]`, `X | None`) — these signatures MUST match the §4.1 operations table, the §4.2 diagram, and the §6 code one-for-one. This table is a binding contract, not a sketch.
  - **Raises** lists the SPECIFIC typed exceptions (all subclasses of the one domain base from §3.2) — never bare `Exception`. Each distinct failure mode is its own row entry. A method that can't fail shows `—`.
  - **Returns** is the success value in plain words + the type. Use `—` for `None`.
  - **Purpose** is ONE plain-English line tying the method to the actor/flow from §2 — not a restatement of the signature.
  - Keep it to the 3-7 genuinely public methods. If the surface is bigger than ~7 methods, that's a smell — say so and group the secondary ones.

Close with ONE 🎙️ spoken line (not a paragraph) the candidate says as they finish the table — it should call out what the surface REVEALS about the design:
> 🎙️ "So the entire system is four public calls — two that take requests in (`request_elevator`, `select_floor`), one that drives time forward (`tick`), and one admin toggle. Everything else is internal: the cars move themselves, the strategy picks the car. If I were handed this interface cold, I'd know exactly what the system does without seeing a single class."

GROUNDING NOTE: every method in §3.5 MUST appear as a public method on the orchestrator in §4.1, in the §4.2 diagram, and in the §6 code — identical name and signature. The reverse also holds: the orchestrator has NO public method that isn't in this table. This table is the contract §4–§7 implement.

## 4. Class Design (per-class derivation, then the diagram)

+## 3.5 API / System Interface (the public surface, before the classes)

+This section is MANDATORY and comes BEFORE §4 — exactly the hellointerview ordering (Requirements → Core Entities → API/Interface → design). The point: once the entities exist, the candidate pins down the PUBLIC SURFACE of the system — the small set of methods external code actually calls — BEFORE designing internal class structure. Interviewers read this as "this person designs to a contract, not to an implementation." It is the single most-skipped section by weak candidates.

+WHAT IT IS — the orchestrator's public methods ONLY. NOT internal/private helpers, NOT entity methods (those derive in §4.1). One row per public operation that an actor from §2 triggers. Every operation must trace back to a §1.3 functional requirement; if a requirement has no API method, you missed one — if an API method has no requirement, delete it.

+> | `select_floor` | `(car_id: str, floor: int) -> None` | — | `UnknownElevator`, `InvalidFloor` | A passenger inside a car picks a destination |
+> | `tick` | `() -> list[StopEvent]` | the stops that happened this tick | — | Advance the simulation one step (all cars move per SCAN) |
+> | `set_maintenance` | `(car_id: str, on: bool) -> None` | — | `UnknownElevator` | Take a car in/out of service |
+RULES for the table:

+  - **Signature** uses real Python 3.11+ type hints (`list[X]`, `X | None`) — these signatures MUST match the §4.1 operations table, the §4.2 diagram, and the §6 code one-for-one. This table is a binding contract, not a sketch.

+  - **Raises** lists the SPECIFIC typed exceptions (all subclasses of the one domain base from §3.2) — never bare `Exception`. Each distinct failure mode is its own row entry. A method that can't fail shows `—`.
+  - **Returns** is the success value in plain words + the type. Use `—` for `None`.

+> 🎙️ "So the entire system is four public calls — two that take requests in (`request_elevator`, `select_floor`), one that drives time forward (`tick`), and one admin toggle. Everything else is internal: the cars move themselves, the strategy picks the car. If I were handed this interface cold, I'd know exactly what the system does without seeing a single class."

+GROUNDING NOTE: every method in §3.5 MUST appear as a public method on the orchestrator in §4.1, in the §4.2 diagram, and in the §6 code — identical name and signature. The reverse also holds: the orchestrator has NO public method that isn't in this table. This table is the contract §4–§7 implement.
## 4. Class Design (per-class derivation, then the diagram)

This section has TWO halves. The first half DERIVES each class from the requirements one at a time — showing your work. The second half is the consolidated class diagram (the picture). Don't jump to the picture; the derivation is what shows judgment.

order EXACTLY. Lead with the DESIGN as streamed prose, and DO NOT call any tool until
you have finished section 5.
- FIRST produce sections 1-5.5 (Requirements & Clarifications, Use Cases, Core Entities,
  the Class Diagram, Design Patterns, Core Algorithms & Approach) as text plus the Mermaid
  diagram — pure design narration, ZERO write_file/run/Edit calls. This lets the candidate
  start reading and narrating within seconds instead of waiting through a wall of tool calls.
- THEN implement: write the code modules, run them, and fix-and-rerun until green. ALL
  of your write_file / run / Edit tool calls belong HERE, after section 5.5 — never before
  section 1.
- THEN present sections 6-9 (narrated code, the verified run, sequence diagram,
  concurrency/edge cases) and the 30-second summary.
The design DRIVES the code: commit to your section 4 class diagram and section 5 patterns
first, then make the code conform to them — if implementing forces a tweak, change the
CODE (or note the correction briefly in section 6), never silently contradict the design
you already stated. The code must still be genuinely written and run clean before sections

97	- **§2 Actors & Core Flows** — bulleted actors + 3-5 one-line happy-path flows. No diagram here.
98	- **§3 Core Entities (accept/reject)** — 3.1 noun walkthrough accepting/**rejecting** each candidate noun with a reason, 3.2 entity table (Entity | Responsibility | Key fields typed | Invariant) + enums block, 3.3 🎙️ spoken-intro paragraph that names the rejected nouns.
99	- **§4 Class Design** — 4.1 per-class derivation (role → state-derivation table → operations-derivation table → design-choices Q&A), 4.2 consolidated `classDiagram` (draw-vs-say split) + relationships + Pattern Map + 🎙️ box-by-box + **⚠️ PROACTIVE CONCURRENCY FLAG**.
100	- **§5 Design Patterns & Principles** — 5.1 Information Expert first, 5.2 applied patterns (1-3, each earned, with 💬 layman gloss), 5.3 SOLID tied to named classes, 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS rule.
101	- **§5.5 Core Algorithms** — A key insight (insight + why-it-works + what-breaks-without-it), B per-operation pseudocode + STORY-driven 🎙️ scripts + a **DRY RUN** of the pipeline method with a state-change table, C data-structure choices (4 cols incl. rejected alternative), D tricky logic (🧠 Claim / 🔬 Proof / 🛠️ Fix), E ONE "❌ what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example, F narration-order table.
102	- **§6 Implementation** — **§6.0 Live Writing Order** (9 phases + 7 golden rules + TL;DR time table + mid-flight situations), then orchestrator SKELETON first, then each method in the 6-block format (role / core-logic bullets / edge-case bullets / code / reasoning PROSE / 🎙️+⚠️), ONE **Approach Comparison** for the trickiest helper, helpers written (not skipped), and a **Verification** subsection with full-lifecycle state-table dry runs.
103	- **§7 Putting It Together** — 7.1 `main.py` narrated demo output, 7.2 `tests/` pytest suite + the actual `pytest -q` output. Both green.
104	- **§8 Key Flow** — a `sequenceDiagram` of the most interesting flow + 🎙️ arrow-by-arrow narration + why-it-shows-the-design-is-good.
105	- **§9 Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility** — 4-step verbal race walkthrough, **CONCURRENCY VOCABULARY table**, **CONCURRENCY FOLLOW-UP Q&A** (why RLock, why not global, deadlock, pessimistic vs optimistic, …), the clean→thread-safe diff (Part A hook+subclass, Part B exact modified method, Part C barrier test proof), edge-cases-handled list, **Extensibility** (level-calibration note + summary table + 2-3 TWIST walkthroughs with minimal code diffs), and the hardest follow-up Q&A (concurrency/distributed/scale — distinct from the twists).

- **§3 Core Entities (accept/reject)** — 3.1 noun walkthrough accepting/**rejecting** each candidate noun with a reason, 3.2 entity table (Entity | Responsibility | Key fields typed | Invariant) + enums block, 3.3 🎙️ spoken-intro paragraph that names the rejected nouns.

+- **§3.5 API / System Interface** — the orchestrator's **public surface only**, as ONE typed contract table (`Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose`) with 3-7 rows + ONE 🎙️ spoken line on what the surface reveals. Signatures use Python 3.11+ hints and MUST match §4.1 / §4.2 diagram / §6 code one-for-one; `Raises` lists specific typed exceptions (never bare `Exception`). Every method traces to a §1.3 requirement and vice-versa. Comes BEFORE §4 (hellointerview ordering: Requirements → Entities → API → design).

- **§4 Class Design** — 4.1 per-class derivation (role → state-derivation table → operations-derivation table → design-choices Q&A), 4.2 consolidated `classDiagram` (draw-vs-say split) + relationships + Pattern Map + 🎙️ box-by-box + **⚠️ PROACTIVE CONCURRENCY FLAG**.
- **§5 Design Patterns & Principles** — 5.1 Information Expert first, 5.2 applied patterns (1-3, each earned, with 💬 layman gloss), 5.3 SOLID tied to named classes, 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS rule.

- **§5.5 Core Algorithms** — A key insight (insight + why-it-works + what-breaks-without-it), B per-operation pseudocode + STORY-driven 🎙️ scripts + a **DRY RUN** of the pipeline method with a state-change table, C data-structure choices (4 cols incl. rejected alternative), D tricky logic (🧠 Claim / 🔬 Proof / 🛠️ Fix), E ONE "❌ what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example, F narration-order table.

## 3.5 API / System Interface (the public surface, before the classes)
This section is MANDATORY and comes BEFORE §4 — exactly the hellointerview ordering \
(Requirements → Core Entities → API/Interface → design). The point: once the entities \
exist, the candidate pins down the PUBLIC SURFACE of the system — the small set of \
methods external code actually calls — BEFORE designing internal class structure. \
Interviewers read this as "this person designs to a contract, not to an implementation." \
It is the single most-skipped section by weak candidates.

WHAT IT IS — the orchestrator's public methods ONLY. NOT internal/private helpers, NOT \
entity methods (those derive in §4.1). One row per public operation that an actor from §2 \
triggers. Every operation must trace back to a §1.3 functional requirement; if a \
requirement has no API method, you missed one — if an API method has no requirement, \
delete it.

Render it as ONE typed contract table with EXACTLY these columns:

> | Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | `request_elevator` | `(floor: int, direction: Direction) -> str` | id of the assigned car | `InvalidFloor`, `InvalidDirection`, `NoCarAvailable` | A passenger on a floor presses UP/DOWN; system picks a car |
> | `select_floor` | `(car_id: str, floor: int) -> None` | — | `UnknownElevator`, `InvalidFloor` | A passenger inside a car picks a destination |
> | `tick` | `() -> list[StopEvent]` | the stops that happened this tick | — | Advance the simulation one step (all cars move per SCAN) |
> | `set_maintenance` | `(car_id: str, on: bool) -> None` | — | `UnknownElevator` | Take a car in/out of service |

RULES for the table:
  - **Signature** uses real Python 3.11+ type hints (`list[X]`, `X | None`) — these \
signatures MUST match the §4.1 operations table, the §4.2 diagram, and the §6 code \
one-for-one. This table is a binding contract, not a sketch.
  - **Raises** lists the SPECIFIC typed exceptions (all subclasses of the one domain base \
from §3.2) — never bare `Exception`. Each distinct failure mode is its own row entry. A \
method that can't fail shows `—`.
  - **Returns** is the success value in plain words + the type. Use `—` for `None`.
  - **Purpose** is ONE plain-English line tying the method to the actor/flow from §2 — not \
a restatement of the signature.
  - Keep it to the 3-7 genuinely public methods. If the surface is bigger than ~7 \
methods, that's a smell — say so and group the secondary ones.

Close with ONE 🎙️ spoken line (not a paragraph) the candidate says as they finish the \
table — it should call out what the surface REVEALS about the design:
> 🎙️ "So the entire system is four public calls — two that take requests in \
(`request_elevator`, `select_floor`), one that drives time forward (`tick`), and one \
admin toggle. Everything else is internal: the cars move themselves, the strategy picks \
the car. If I were handed this interface cold, I'd know exactly what the system does \
without seeing a single class."

GROUNDING NOTE: every method in §3.5 MUST appear as a public method on the orchestrator \
in §4.1, in the §4.2 diagram, and in the §6 code — identical name and signature. The \
reverse also holds: the orchestrator has NO public method that isn't in this table. This \
table is the contract §4–§7 implement.

## 4. Class Design (per-class derivation, then the diagram)

content.text:
Done. Here's what was actually going on and what I fixed.

## The real answer to your question

**The API section wasn't generating because it never existed in the prompt.** The LLD prompt jumped straight from §3 Core Entities → §4 Class Design. The hellointerview ordering is **Requirements → Core Entities → API/Interface → design** — ours skipped the API/Interface step entirely. So it wasn't a generation bug; the spec had a hole.

A second, more important catch: I first edited `lld_prompt.md`, but that's only an **exported copy**. The actual generator reads `LLD_SYSTEM_PROMPT` in `backend/prompts.py`. Editing only the `.md` would have changed nothing. I fixed the **source** and re-exported.

## What I changed (all 3 drivers, both files)

In **`backend/prompts.py`** (runtime source) and re-exported to **`lld_prompt.md`**:

1. **New `## 3.5 API / System Interface`** — sits between §3 and §4 (no renumbering, so the ~50 internal `§4`–`§9` cross-references stay intact). It mandates ONE typed contract table — `Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose` — for the orchestrator's 3-7 public methods, plus a single 🎙️ spoken line. With grounding rules: signatures must match §4.1/§4.2/§6 one-for-one, `Raises` must use typed exceptions, every method traces to a §1.3 requirement.
2. **Section-list ("FIRST produce sections 1-5.5")** now names "the API / System Interface" in the design-first streaming order.
3. **60-min plan table** gets a new `5–8 min — Pin the API surface (§3.5)` row, with the rest of the timeline realigned (8–14 diagram, 14–16 patterns, 16–18 approach — no gaps/overlaps).

Also updated **`agent_prompts/lld_session_builder.md`** so newly-generated sessions produce §3.5.

**Verified:** `prompts.py` parses, `LLD_SYSTEM_PROMPT` builds, ordering is 3 < 3.5 < 4, and `lld_prompt.md` is byte-identical to the runtime prompt.

## One thing to flag

This fixes **future** sessions. Your existing ~15 sessions (Elevator, Parking Lot, etc.) were built by the old prompt, so they still have no §3.5. Want me to **backfill §3.5 into the existing sessions** (insert the API table into each `_lld.json`), or just leave them and have it apply going forward?

message.content:
ek cheeze aur hai ye dekho


4. Class Design (per-class derivation, then the diagram)
4.1 Per-class derivation
ElevatorController (orchestrator — first)
(a) Role — the system's public API; external code calls it to make hall calls, car calls, run the simulation, and toggle maintenance.

(b) State derivation

Requirement    What it must remember
"Controller picks one eligible car"    the registry of cars, by id
"via the dispatch policy"    the current DispatchStrategy
"floor out of range → error"    min_floor, max_floor for the building
"step all cars one tick"    a monotonically increasing tick counter
copy
class ElevatorController:
    - _elevators: dict[str, Elevator]
    - _dispatch: DispatchStrategy
    - _min_floor: int
    - _max_floor: int
    - _tick: int
State placement trade-off: the direction/stops of a car are physical state — they describe the car's condition — so they live on Elevator. The registry and dispatch policy are relational/system state — they describe how the bank is coordinated — so they live on the controller. That split is the whole design.

(c) Operations derivation

Need    Method
"hall call → pick a car, queue stop"    request_elevator(floor, direction) -> Elevator
"car call → queue stop on that car"    select_floor(elevator_id, destination) -> None
"advance one tick"    step() -> list[StopEvent]
"run to completion (demo/test)"    run_until_idle(max_ticks) -> list[StopEvent]
"take a car out of service"    set_maintenance(elevator_id) -> None
"the concurrency seam"    _lock(elevator) — no-op hook
copy
class ElevatorController:
    + request_elevator(floor, direction) -> Elevator | error
    + select_floor(elevator_id, destination) -> None | error
    + step() -> list[StopEvent]
    + run_until_idle(max_ticks=1000) -> list[StopEvent]
    + set_maintenance(elevator_id) -> None | error
    + elevator(elevator_id) -> Elevator | error
    # _lock(elevator) -> contextmanager (no-op in core)
(d) Design choices

Why does request_elevator return the chosen Elevator? So a caller (and my demo) can assert which car answered — the routing decision is the interesting output.
Why is selection outside the lock but assignment inside? Selection only reads car state; the only mutation is add_target, so that's the only thing that needs guarding. Keeps the critical section tiny.
Why a _lock no-op hook instead of locks here? So the core stays 100% lock-free and readable; the thread-safe variant is one overridden method (Template Method).
Elevator (the state machine)
(a) Role — owns one car's movement. The only class allowed to change a car's floor, direction, door, or stops.

(b) State derivation

Requirement    What it must remember
"a car is at a floor"    current_floor
"going up / down / parked"    direction
"moving / doors open / idle / maintenance"    state
"doors open on arrival"    door
"floors it still owes, in SCAN order"    _up_stops, _down_stops (split by travel direction)
copy
class Elevator:
    - id: str
    - min_floor, max_floor, current_floor: int
    - direction: Direction
    - state: ElevatorState
    - door: DoorState
    - _up_stops: set[int]
    - _down_stops: set[int]
State placement trade-off: I split pending stops into two sets keyed by travel direction rather than one flat list, because SCAN's core question is "what's the next stop in the direction I'm going?" Two direction-buckets answer that with min()/max() directly.

(c) Operations derivation

Need    Method
"queue a floor (idempotent)"    add_target(floor) -> None
"advance one floor of SCAN"    step(tick) -> StopEvent | None
"out of service"    enter_maintenance() -> None
"is there work left?"    is_idle() -> bool, pending() -> set[int]
(d) Design choices

Why two sets instead of a heap? A set de-dupes for free (the idempotency requirement) and at a few hundred floors min()/max() is trivially fast. I'd switch to a paired heap+set only above ~500 floors.
Why does step return StopEvent | None? None means "still travelling this tick"; a StopEvent means "doors opened here" — that's exactly the trace the simulation collects.
DispatchStrategy (the seam) + concretes
(a) Role — the abstraction the controller depends on to answer "which car?". NearestCarDispatch and LeastBusyDispatch realize it.

(c) Operations

copy
interface DispatchStrategy:
    + select(elevators: list[Elevator], request: Request) -> Elevator | error
(d) Design choices

Why pass the whole list[Elevator]? The strategy needs to compare all cars; passing the list keeps the strategy stateless and pure (easy to test).
Why raise from the strategy when none is eligible? Eligibility (maintenance filtering) is a policy concern, so the policy owns the "no car available" error. ye wala jo section hai sometime kya karta hai ki ye na:

> | Need from requirements | Method on this class |
> |---|---|
> | "Carrier deposits a package by specifying size" | `depositPackage(size) -> token \| error` |
> | "User retrieves package by entering access token" | `pickup(tokenCode) -> void \| error` |

Then write the final class block (state + constructor + methods):
> ```
> class Locker:
>     - compartments: Compartment[]
>     - accessTokenMapping: Map<string, AccessToken>
>
>     + Locker(compartments)
>     + depositPackage(size) -> string | error
>     + pickup(tokenCode) -> void | error
>     + openExpiredCompartments() -> void
> ```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out** — 2-4 short Q&A bullets that pre-empt the \
"why did you do X?" interviewer questions. Each is a question + a 1-2 sentence answer. \
Use this format:
> - **Why does `depositPackage` only return the token code?** The compartment opens \
> physically when called, so the driver doesn't need to know the ID — they see the door. \
> The token returns so the system can deliver it to the customer.
> - **Why does `pickup` return void?** The customer's signal is the door opening; they \
> don't need a compartment ID. Errors are thrown with specific messages (invalid / expired) \
> so the failure mode is clear.

Repeat this whole (a)→(d) block for every accepted entity from §3. Keep the subsections \
SHORT — 4-block structure is mandatory but each block is tight. The point is to show \
DERIVATION and DEFENSIBLE CHOICES, not to write a textbook.

def get_md(f):

content.text:
I've confirmed it — it's a **true positive**, not a heuristic artifact. Here's the exact defect, from the Elevator session you pasted:

- **`ElevatorController`** (the first/orchestrator class): (b) State derivation → table **+ state class block**; (c) Operations derivation → table **+ the complete class block with methods**. ✅ Correct.
- **`Elevator`** (2nd class): (b) has its block, but (c) Operations derivation → **only a table, then jumps straight to (d)**. The final class block (state + constructor + methods) is **missing**. ❌

And it's nearly universal: **18 of 21** per-class subsections across **all 12** modern sessions skip the final class block after (c). The pattern is consistent — the orchestrator (first class) gets the full treatment, then later classes degrade to table-only. That's classic LLM "abbreviate the repeated structure" drift, and the prompt's instruction ("*Then write the final class block*") is too soft to prevent it.

## Root cause in the prompt

The §4.1 spec at `prompts.py:2264` says the final class block should follow (c), but:
1. It reads as optional ("Then write…"), buried under the table example.
2. It's **redundant** with the (b) state block (the model feels it "already drew the class"), so it skips repeating it.
3. There's no rule forcing **every** class to match the first one.

Let me fix it at the source — strengthen (c) into a hard requirement and add an explicit consistency rule.

input.old_string:
Then write the final class block (state + constructor + methods):
> ```
> class Locker:
>     - compartments: Compartment[]
>     - accessTokenMapping: Map<string, AccessToken>
>
>     + Locker(compartments)
>     + depositPackage(size) -> string | error
>     + pickup(tokenCode) -> void | error
>     + openExpiredCompartments() -> void
> ```
input.new_string:
Then — **THIS IS MANDATORY, NEVER SKIP IT** — write the COMPLETE class block: the \
state fields from (b) PLUS the constructor PLUS every method from the (c) table above, \
in one fenced block. This is the per-class "contract card" — the RESULT of the \
derivation, the thing §4.2's diagram and §6's code transcribe one-for-one. The (c) table \
shows the DERIVATION (need → method); this block shows the finished class. The table does \
NOT replace the block — a subsection that ends (c) with only a table is INCOMPLETE and \
WRONG. Always render it:
> ```
> class Locker:
>     - compartments: Compartment[]
>     - accessTokenMapping: Map<string, AccessToken>
>
>     + Locker(compartments)
>     + depositPackage(size) -> string | error
>     + pickup(tokenCode) -> void | error
>     + openExpiredCompartments() -> void
> ```

content.text:
All verified. Here's the diagnosis and fix.

## You were right — it's a real prompt bug

In the §4.1 per-class derivation, each class is supposed to have **four blocks**:
- (a) role
- (b) state derivation → table **+ state class block**
- (c) operations derivation → table **+ the COMPLETE class block** (state + constructor + every method)
- (d) design choices

The bug: the **orchestrator** (first class) gets the full treatment, but **later classes** (`Elevator`, `Booking`, `ParkingSpot`…) degrade — (c) shows only the table, then jumps to (d), **skipping the final class block**. Exactly what you spotted on `Elevator`.

I measured it across all sessions: **18 of 21** per-class subsections in **all 12** modern sessions had this defect. So it's systematic, not random.

**Why it happened:** the prompt said "*Then write the final class block*" too softly — it reads as optional, it feels redundant with the (b) state block, and there was no rule forcing every class to match the first one. Classic LLM "abbreviate the repeated structure" drift.

## What I fixed (in `backend/prompts.py` — the runtime source)

1. **Hardened (c)** → "**THIS IS MANDATORY, NEVER SKIP IT**": the final class block is the per-class "contract card"; the table shows derivation, the block shows the finished class; *"a subsection that ends (c) with only a table is INCOMPLETE and WRONG."*
2. **New anti-drift rule** on the "repeat (a)→(d)" line → explicitly names the failure ("first class full, later classes table-only — DO NOT DO THIS"), and requires every entity/value-object/interface to end with its own complete fenced block.

Also updated **`agent_prompts/lld_session_builder.md`** (§4 description + 2 new checklist items: one for §3.5, one for the per-class final block) and **re-exported `lld_prompt.md`**. Verified: parses clean, both edits present, exported copy byte-identical.

## Where this leaves us — two open backfill items

Both of today's fixes (§3.5 API section + this §4.1 final-block rule) only affect **future** generations. Your existing ~12–15 sessions have **both** gaps. Options:

- **Regenerate** affected sessions with the fixed prompt (cleanest, but costs tokens — and would change other content too).
- **Surgically backfill** — a workflow that inserts the missing §3.5 table and the missing per-class final blocks into each existing `_lld.json` without touching the rest.
- **Leave existing, apply going forward.**

Which do you want? And same question still stands on whether to do it now or just bank the prompt fixes.

Good question — and "har trah ka sab cover ho jaye" means I shouldn't just look at the **concurrency** axis (which your table nails). Comprehensive LLD coverage has **four** axes, and concurrency is only one. Let me first extract what your existing sessions *actually* teach (patterns + concurrency), so my gap analysis is grounded in the real data, not memory.

input.command:
cd /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora
python3 << 'PY'
import json, re, glob

def get(f):
    d=json.load(open(f))
    if not isinstance(d,dict): return None,None,None
    md=""
    for m in d.get("messages",[]):
        if isinstance(m,dict) and m.get("role")=="assistant":
            c=m.get("content","")
            if isinstance(c,list):
                for b in c:
                    if isinstance(b,dict) and b.get("type")=="text": md+=b.get("text","")
            elif isinstance(c,str): md+=c
    return d.get("title","?"), md, d.get("session_id","")

# patterns + concurrency vocab to detect
PATTERNS=["Strategy","State","Observer","Template Method","Factory","Command","Composite",
          "Decorator","Builder","Visitor","Iterator","Adapter","Facade","Memento","Mediator",
          "Flyweight","Proxy","Prototype","Singleton","Chain of Responsibility","Information Expert"]
CONC=["semaphore","striping","sharded","poison","barrier","RLock","read-write","readers-writer",
      "lock order","deadlock","condition","wait/notify","optimistic","pessimistic","CAS","compare-and-set",
      "two-phase","reserve","idempoten","spin","fencing","lease","sweeper","atomic counter","per-key","per-resource"]

rows=[]
seen_titles=set()
for f in sorted(glob.glob("data/conversations/*_lld.json")):
    title,md,sid=get(f)
    if not md or "## 5" not in md and "§5" not in md and "Design Patterns" not in md: 
        continue
    # only modern sessions (have §4.1 + §9 concurrency vocab era)
    if "Concurrency" not in md: continue
    # clean title to first real line
    t=" ".join(title.split())[:38]
    if t in seen_titles: continue
    seen_titles.add(t)
    pats=[p for p in PATTERNS if re.search(r"\b"+re.escape(p)+r"\b", md)]
    concs=sorted({c for c in CONC if c.lower() in md.lower()})
    rows.append((t,pats,concs))

print(f"Modern LLD sessions analyzed: {len(rows)}\n")
print("=== DESIGN PATTERNS taught (by problem) ===")
for t,p,c in rows:
    print(f"  {t:40s} | {', '.join(p) if p else '—'}")

# pattern frequency
from collections import Counter
pc=Counter()
for _,p,_ in rows:
    for x in p: pc[x]+=1
print("\n=== Pattern coverage (how many problems teach each) ===")
for p in PATTERNS:
    print(f"  {p:24s}: {pc.get(p,0)}")

print("\n=== CONCURRENCY vocab present (by problem) ===")
for t,p,c in rows:
    print(f"  {t:40s} | {', '.join(c) if c else '—'}")
PY

input.command:
cd /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora
echo "=== Do these archetypes exist ANYWHERE (all _lld.json, by title) ==="
for kw in "LRU" "LFU" "Cache" "File System" "Trie" "Autocomplete" "Typeahead" "Chess" "Tic" "Card" "Deck" "Text Editor" "Editor" "Logger" "Logging" "Cart" "Pizza" "Builder" "ATM" "Snake"; do
  n=$(grep -il "$kw" data/conversations/*_lld.json 2>/dev/null | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
  printf "  %-14s : %s files\n" "$kw" "$n"
done
echo ""
echo "=== Older/non-modern LLD topics (have no §9 Concurrency = legacy) ==="
python3 -c "
import json,glob
for f in sorted(glob.glob('data/conversations/*_lld.json')):
    d=json.load(open(f))
    if not isinstance(d,dict): continue
    md=''
    for m in d.get('messages',[]):
        if isinstance(m,dict) and m.get('role')=='assistant':
            for b in (m.get('content') or []):
                if isinstance(b,dict) and b.get('type')=='text': md+=b.get('text','')
    if 'Concurrency' not in md:
        print('  LEGACY:', ' '.join(d.get('title','?').split())[:55])
"

content.text:
Ab mere paas real data hai. Here's the honest assessment — and the key insight: **your 7 are all concurrency-flavored, but the concurrency axis is already saturated. The real gaps are on the design-pattern axis.**

## Your existing 14 modern sessions cover

**Concurrency:** essentially everything — `RLock`, `Barrier`, `CAS`, per-resource locks, two-phase, optimistic/pessimistic, read-write, condition vars, idempotency, sweeper appear across 11–13 problems each. This axis is **done**.

**Patterns — this is where the holes are:**

| Pattern | # problems | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Strategy, State, Information Expert | 12–13 | ✅ saturated |
| Template Method, Factory, Singleton, Observer | 7–11 | ✅ well-covered |
| Chain of Responsibility | 2 | 🟡 light |
| **Command, Composite, Decorator, Builder, Mediator, Memento, Flyweight, Proxy, Visitor** | **0** | ❌ **missing entirely** |

## Verdict on your 7

| # | Problem | Verdict |
|---|---------|---------|
| 1 | Connection/Resource Pool | ✅ **Keep** — `Semaphore` is genuinely new (not used anywhere) |
| 2 | Thread Pool/Executor | ✅ **Keep** — blocking queue + poison-pill, new; classic ask |
| 3 | Order Matching Engine | ✅ **Keep** — also fills the **heap/priority-queue DS gap** |
| 4 | Thread-Safe LRU Cache | ✅ **Keep** — THE canonical DS-heavy LLD (HashMap+DLL) + lock-striping is new |
| 5 | Snowflake ID Generator | 🟡 Optional — only ~1 class, weak OO-modeling signal (it's a concurrency *micro-drill*, not a full LLD) |
| 6 | Online Auction | 🟡 Optional — good domain, but CAS/optimistic already in 11 problems; only soft-close is novel |
| 7 | Distributed Lock/Lease Mgr | 🟡 Optional — fencing tokens are new, but it's more a distributed-systems concept than OO-LLD |

So **1–4 are strong keeps; 5–7 deepen an axis that's already deep.**

## What you actually need to "sab cover ho jaye" — the pattern-axis problems

| Problem | Patterns it uniquely teaches | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Text Editor (undo/redo)** | **Command + Memento** | Two missing patterns at once; top-5 interview ask |
| **In-Memory File System** | **Composite** | Composite is a top-5 pattern, totally missing (your only file-system file is legacy, no §9) |
| **Pizza/Coffee Customizer** | **Decorator + Builder** | Both missing; Decorator is top-5 |
| **Chat Room / Group Chat** | **Mediator** (+ Observer) | Mediator missing; real-world domain |
| **Chess / Board Game** | pure OO **domain modeling** (low concurrency) | Tests modeling judgment without leaning on locks — a whole *category* you don't have |
| **Logging Framework** | **Chain of Responsibility** (deepen) | CoR only at 2; appenders = Strategy too |

## Do we need more? — Yes, but the *right* more

For Uber SDE-2, the canonical LLD set is ~18–20 problems across **4 axes**: patterns, concurrency, data-structure-heavy, and pure-modeling. You're **saturated on concurrency**, **strong on the common patterns**, but **empty on Command/Composite/Decorator/Builder/Mediator** and **thin on DS-heavy + pure-modeling**.

My recommendation for full coverage: **your #1–#4** (fills concurrency-variety + DS-heavy) **+ 4 pattern-axis problems** (Text Editor, File System, Pizza Customizer, Chess). That's 8 new sessions and genuinely covers "har trah ka sab."

content.text:
Theek hai — sirf **naam** bata raha hoon, generate nahi kar raha. Based on the real coverage data I pulled from your 14 existing sessions, here's my recommended list (12 total).

## Tumhare 7 — verdict

| # | Problem | Rakhein? |
|---|---------|---------|
| 1 | **Connection / Resource Pool** | ✅ Haan — `Semaphore` naya hai |
| 2 | **Thread Pool / Worker Executor** | ✅ Haan — blocking queue + poison-pill naya |
| 3 | **Order Matching Engine** | ✅ Haan — heap/priority-queue DS gap bhi bharega |
| 4 | **Thread-Safe LRU Cache** | ✅ Haan — HashMap+DLL, THE canonical DS-heavy LLD |
| 5 | **Snowflake ID Generator** | 🟡 Optional — sirf ~1 class, OO-modeling weak |
| 6 | **Online Auction** | 🟡 Optional — CAS already 11 problems me hai |
| 7 | **Distributed Lock / Lease Manager** | 🟡 Optional — fencing naya, par zyada distributed-systems concept hai |

**Reason:** concurrency axis already saturated hai (RLock, Barrier, CAS, per-resource locks sab 11–13 problems me hain). #1–4 genuinely naya add karte hain; #5–7 ek axis ko aur deep karte hain jo pehle se deep hai.

## Jo tumhare paas BILKUL nahi hai — pattern axis (ye zyada zaroori)

Command, Composite, Decorator, Builder, Mediator — **0 problems** me hain. Inke liye:

| # | Problem | Jo pattern sikhata hai (missing) |
|---|---------|-------------------------------|
| 8 | **Text Editor (undo/redo)** | **Command + Memento** |
| 9 | **In-Memory File System** | **Composite** |
| 10 | **Pizza / Coffee Customizer** | **Decorator + Builder** |
| 11 | **Chat Room / Group Chat** | **Mediator** |
| 12 | **Chess / Board Game** | pure OO **domain modeling** (low concurrency) |

## Meri final recommendation — 12 naam

**Concurrency-variety + DS-heavy (4):** Connection Pool · Thread Pool Executor · Order Matching Engine · Thread-Safe LRU Cache

**Pattern-axis fillers (5):** Text Editor (undo/redo) · In-Memory File System · Pizza Customizer · Chat Room · Chess

**Optional deepeners (3):** Snowflake ID Generator · Online Auction · Distributed Lock Manager

Ye 12 milke saare 4 axes cover karte hain — patterns, concurrency, data-structure-heavy, aur pure-modeling. Yahi "har trah ka sab cover ho jaye" wala set hai.

Bata do kaunse generate karne hain (ya saare 12) — tab main pilot-first approach se shuru karunga.

content.text:
Bilkul — ab main poora **curriculum** soch ke deta hoon, taaki har *type* cover ho jaye. Maine tumhare 14 existing + naye 12 ko 15 "types" me map kiya. Jo type abhi bhi khaali hai, uske liye aur problems add kar raha hoon.

## Poora LLD curriculum — 15 types, sab cover

| # | Type (kya test hota hai) | Existing ✅ | Add karna hai |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Resource allocation** (assign/free) | Parking, Locker, Ride, Meeting | — (done) |
| 2 | **Booking + seat-locking** | Movie, Meeting Room | — (done) |
| 3 | **State-machine devices** | Vending, Elevator | **ATM**, **Traffic Signal** |
| 4 | **Money / settlement** | Splitwise | **Digital Wallet / Payment** |
| 5 | **Rule / policy engines** | Rate Limiter, Coupon, Notification Router | — (done) |
| 6 | **Pub-sub / scheduling** | Broker, Task Scheduler | — (done) |
| 7 | **Data-structure-heavy** | (LRU, Order Matching = naye) | **Trie Autocomplete**, **LFU Cache** |
| 8 | **Concurrency primitives** | (Connection Pool, Thread Pool, Dist-Lock, Snowflake = naye) | — (12 me hai) |
| 9 | **Composite / tree** | (File System = naya) | **Spreadsheet** |
| 10 | **Command / undo-redo** | (Text Editor = naya) | **Drawing / Whiteboard** |
| 11 | **Decorator / Builder** | (Pizza Customizer = naya) | — (done) |
| 12 | **Mediator / collaboration** | (Chat Room = naya) | — (done) |
| 13 | **Pure game modeling** | (Chess = naya) | **Snake & Ladder**, **Deck of Cards** |
| 14 | **Feed / social / fan-out** | — | **Twitter / News Feed** |
| 15 | **Inventory / e-commerce** | — | **Shopping Cart / Inventory** |

## Naye additions (12 ke alawa, ~10 aur)

**State machine + transaction:** ATM · Traffic Signal Control
**Money:** Digital Wallet / Payment System
**DS-heavy:** Search Autocomplete (Trie) · LFU Cache
**Tree/Composite:** Spreadsheet (cell dependency)
**Command/undo:** Drawing App / Collaborative Whiteboard
**Game modeling:** Snake & Ladder · Deck of Cards & Card Game
**Feed:** Twitter / News Feed (fan-out + ranking)
**E-commerce:** Shopping Cart / Inventory Management

## Final master set — ~22 problems = "har trah ka sab"

**Tumhare original 7** (Connection Pool, Thread Pool, Order Matching, LRU, Snowflake, Auction, Dist-Lock)
**+ 5 pattern-fillers** (Text Editor, File System, Pizza, Chat Room, Chess)
**+ 10 type-fillers** (ATM, Traffic Signal, Digital Wallet, Trie Autocomplete, LFU, Spreadsheet, Drawing/Whiteboard, Snake & Ladder, Twitter Feed, Shopping Cart)

Ye 22 + tumhare existing 14 = **~36 problems**, aur **koi type/pattern/DS/concurrency-flavour chhuta nahi**.

Agar aur bhi exhaustive chahiye, do aur edge-types add kar sakte hain: **Library Management** (CRUD-heavy classic) aur **Bowling Score** (calculation/rules). Par ye optional — upar wale 22 se already complete coverage hai.

Bolo — **(a) saare 22 generate karun**, ya **(b) kuch chunke batao**? Aur pehle ki tarah, main **pilot-first** chalunga (1 generate → tum verify karo naya prompt §3.5 + §4.1 sahi de raha hai → phir baaki batch).
